diagrams.title: Basic Pointer Concept
diagrams.description: Shows how a pointer variable stores the memory address of another variable, demonstrating the fundamental pointer-variable relationship in C++
elements: A memory box representing variable 'int x = 42' with label showing name, value (42), and memory address (e.g., 0x1000)
A memory box representing pointer variable 'int* ptr' with label showing name and memory address (e.g., 0x2000)
An arrow drawn from the pointer box to the variable box, representing that ptr stores the address of x
Text label inside pointer showing the stored address value (0x1000)
diagrams.purpose: Explains the core concept of pointers: they are variables that hold memory addresses rather than actual values, and how dereferencing accesses the pointed-to value

diagrams.title: Reference Variables as Aliases
diagrams.description: Demonstrates that a reference variable is an alias (nickname) for another variable, not a separate memory location like a pointer
elements: A single memory box representing 'int x = 10' at address 0x1000
A reference box labeled 'int& ref = x' pointing to the same box as x
Visual connection showing ref is another name for the SAME memory location
Text annotation: 'ref is x - they share the same memory address'
Demonstration showing both x and ref can modify the same value
diagrams.purpose: Clarifies that references are NOT new variables but simply alternative names for existing variables, distinguishing them fundamentally from pointers

diagrams.title: Pointer vs Reference Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing the differences between pointers and references when accessing and modifying the same original variable
elements: Left side: variable 'int value = 25' at address 0x1000
Middle: pointer 'int* ptr = &value' storing address 0x1000, showing *ptr needed to access value
Right side: reference 'int& ref = value' directly as an alias
Visual arrows: pointer arrow points TO the variable (needs dereferencing), reference arrow merges INTO the variable
Code examples showing: ptr vs *ptr, and ref directly (no dereferencing needed)
Summary table highlighting: pointers can be null, references cannot; pointers can be reassigned, references cannot
diagrams.purpose: Provides a clear visual comparison to help students understand when to use pointers vs references and the syntax differences between them
